Client 2: And I’m Patricia. I’m still working, so I’m mostly thinking about cash flow and, honestly, what happens if something goes sideways.
Advisor: That makes sense. Let’s start with income at the household level—ballpark is fine, then we’ll pin it down. What’s your total gross income per year right now?
Client 2: Combined, it’s 109750 a year gross.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And just so I’m not mixing sources, Thomas, do you have income coming in personally, and Patricia, do you as well?
Client 1: Yes. Mine is 79,800 a year gross.

Client 2: And mine is 29,950 gross annually from work.
Advisor: Perfect. Now switching to spending—what do your total monthly expenses run, all-in? Mortgage or rent, utilities, food, insurance, the usual.
Client 2: We’re pretty steady at 4500 a month.
Advisor: Okay. When you say expenses, does that include any debt payments—credit cards, car loans, student loans—anything like that?
Client 2: No, we don’t have any monthly debt payments. It’s 0.

Advisor: Great—so no loans outstanding. One sec, I’m just noting that down. Now, assets—roughly how much do you have invested outside the house, total investable assets?
Client 1: Total investable assets are 630800.
Advisor: Thanks. Let’s break that into the main buckets so I understand what’s where. Do you have a joint taxable brokerage account?
Client 1: Yes—joint taxable brokerage, and it’s 331,500.

Client 1: Yes, private markets held at a bank—72,500.

Client 2: We use 347,700 for the house value.
Advisor: Perfect. On the debt side, you already said no monthly debt payments—so no mortgage balance, no car loan, nothing like that, correct?
Client 1: Correct—no mortgage, no car loan, nothing. Zero debt.

Advisor: Before we fully wrap, I want to capture a couple of planning assumptions that drive the projections—then we’re done for today.
Advisor: First one’s taxes. Thomas, Patricia—what tax bracket are you generally in right now, the federal marginal bracket you plan around?
Client 1: We plan around 32%.
Advisor: Got it—32%. And that’s the bracket you expect to be in near-term as well, unless something changes.
Client 2: Yeah, 32% is what we use when we’re estimating.

Client 2: TX.
Advisor: TX—thank you. That helps with the state tax assumptions and any estate planning coordination later.
Advisor: Last one is just how you want to work together. Are you looking for this to be managed through an RIA—registered investment advisor relationship—ongoing advice and portfolio management? Or more of a one-time plan?
Client 1: RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Great—thank you. Patricia, same expectation on your side?

Client 2: Yeah, 32% is what we use when we’re estimating.
Advisor: Okay. Next is risk—just so I’m not guessing when we talk portfolio next time. How would you describe your overall risk tolerance as a household?
Client 1: Aggressive.
Advisor: Okay—aggressive. Patricia, does that feel right to you too, even if you’re a little more cautious day-to-day?
Client 2: Yeah—overall it’s aggressive. I’m the one who asks the “what if it drops” questions, but yes, aggressive.

Advisor: Perfect. Let me note that down—one sec.
Advisor: And then logistics: what state do you file from—where’s your primary residence state-wise?
Client 2: TX.
Advisor: TX—thank you. That helps with the state tax assumptions and any estate planning coordination later.
